prophecy-and-persia.script: Welcome to Prophecy and Persia: Cyrus, Islam and the Imam Mahdi. In front of you stands the towering stone gateway of Persepolis, its colossal winged bull-men keeping watch as they have for millennia. This exhibition, presented by the Department for Outreach and Public Relations of the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community UK, follows a single remarkable thread through history, in three parts. First, we meet Cyrus the Great, the Persian emperor whom the Holy Quran may call Dhul-Qarnain. Then we witness the Rise of Islam and Persia's place within it. Finally, we come to the Promised Messiah, the awaited reformer of Persian origin, the Imam Mahdi. The artefacts you will encounter along the way are courtesy of Razwan Baig and the Barlas Foundation. This audio guide will accompany you at every board, and is available in several languages. Take your time, and let the journey begin.

cyrus.script: Cyrus. You are entering Section One, and its purpose is to unravel the true identity of Dhul-Qarnain, the two-horned king mentioned in the Holy Quran. Before you is the carved face of a Persian noble, gazing out from the stone as it has for two and a half thousand years. Over the boards ahead, you will meet Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian Empire, and weigh the case that he, rather than Alexander, was the righteous figure the Quran describes. Watch for his conquests to the west, the east and the north, for his famous cylinder, and for the wall he raised against Gog and Magog. Let us begin with the promise of the Holy Quran itself.

promise-of-the-holy-quran.script: The Promise of the Holy Quran. Shown here in flowing Arabic calligraphy, the Holy Quran is unique. It tells us that God Almighty is the Lord of every realm; of every people, and every nation. This means that no one people is inherently supreme over any others; all human beings are equal before God. Our division into different peoples is not a hierarchy among humanity. Rather, those closest to Allah are simply those who are most righteous. But how can we become righteous? Through following the Prophets. For Islam teaches that all humanity received God's guidance. As the Quran declares, in chapter sixteen, verse thirty-seven: "And We did raise among every people a Messenger with the teaching, 'Worship Allah and shun the Evil One.' Then among them were some whom Allah guided and among them were some who became deserving of ruin." Each people, then, was sent a Prophet; yet over time, people corrupted their religions. At last the time came when humanity could bear a single, universal message: Islam, a universal message for all peoples and all time. And the Quran claims explicitly to be that universal teaching to unite all humanity, declaring in chapter seven, verse one hundred and fifty-nine: "Say, 'O mankind! truly I am a Messenger to you all from Allah to Whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. There is no God but He. He gives life, and He causes death...'" This message was revealed through the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, who was born in five hundred and ninety, received his first revelation of the Quran in six hundred and ten, and saw the Quran completed by six hundred and thirty-two. The message of Islam is simple, and should its spirit ever be lost by the Muslims themselves, Allah promised to rejuvenate it by His own hand: "Verily, We Ourself have sent down this Exhortation, and most surely We will be its Guardian" — chapter fifteen, verse ten. And the story of how this renewal came to pass is inimitably tied up with one particular people: the Persians.

verses.text: And We did raise among every people a Messenger with the teaching, 'Worship Allah and shun the Evil One.' Then among them were some whom Allah guided and among them were some who became deserving of ruin.
Say, 'O mankind! truly I am a Messenger to you all from Allah to Whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. There is no God but He. He gives life, and He causes death...'
Verily, We Ourself have sent down this Exhortation, and most surely We will be its Guardian.

ancient-persia-zoroastrianism.script: Ancient Persia and Zoroastrianism. In front of you rise the ruins of ancient Persepolis at golden hour. First, let us place Persia and Iran in history. The name 'Persia' comes from the Ancient Persian word 'Parsa', the name for what is now the Iranian province of Fars. The inhabitants of this area called themselves the Arya, meaning 'the noble' or 'the civilised', and they called their homeland 'Iran'. Fars, or Parsa, was the centre of Cyrus the Great's Achaemenid Dynasty; and when the Greeks came across this empire, they called their realm Persis, in Latin, Persia. As the Persian Empire spread, the name 'Persians' became applicable to a huge number of peoples living under Persian rule. As for dates: linguistic analysis of the Gathas places Zoroaster in the second millennium before the Common Era, between twelve hundred and one thousand BCE; Greek historians, however, give the sixth century BCE as his birth century; and six hundred BCE is the birth year of Cyrus the Second, the Great. Long before Cyrus, then, came the Prophet Zoroaster. Zarathustra, later known as Zoroaster, was a great prophetic figure in the Iranian tradition. His society worshipped many gods and brutalised their neighbours. Zarathustra was commissioned by Ahura Mazda, the One True God, to spiritually reform his people, and he taught monotheism. In the Gathas, the Zoroastrian scriptures, he proclaimed, as recorded in Yasna forty-five, verse six: "Now, I shall speak of the Greatest One of all, praising with truth Him who is beneficent through His virtuous spirit to those who exist." In the same scriptures, Zarathustra recalls Ahura Mazda questioning him, in Yasna forty-three, verses seven to eight: "How, this day, wouldst thou begin to explain these revelations among thy creatures and thine own?" Then I said to Him first, "I am Zarathustra. If I were able, I would be a true enemy to the deceitful one but a strong support to the Truthful One." Yet he was bitterly opposed by the priests and princes of his land, and was forced to migrate for his faith. In that hour he lamented, in Yasna forty-six, verse one: "To what land shall I turn, and whither turning shall I go? For my kinsmen and my peers have deserted me. Not the people, nor their wicked rulers, favor me. How shall I satisfy Thee, O Ahura Mazda?" In the end, though, he was victorious. His spiritual kingdom became a worldly kingdom through one of the greatest sons of Persia: Cyrus the Great.

verses.text: Now, I shall speak of the Greatest One of all, praising with truth Him who is beneficent through His virtuous spirit to those who exist.

How, this day, wouldst thou begin to explain these revelations among thy creatures and thine own?" Then I said to Him first, "I am Zarathustra. If I were able, I would be a true enemy to the deceitful one but a strong support to the Truthful One.
To what land shall I turn, and whither turning shall I go? For my kinsmen and my peers have deserted me. Not the people, nor their wicked rulers, favor me. How shall I satisfy Thee, O Ahura Mazda?

cyrus-ii-persian-emperor.script: Cyrus the Second, Persian Emperor. This carved relief from Persepolis shows a procession of Persian and Median nobles. Cyrus was born into a world on fire. Between nine hundred and eleven and six hundred and nine BCE, the regional superpower was the Neo-Assyrian empire. They were famous for their viciousness, and dominated the Medes of northwestern Iran and the Babylonians of Southern Mesopotamia. In seven hundred and twenty-two BCE the Assyrians captured the Northern Kingdom of Israel and enslaved them, the so-called lost tribes; and in six hundred and twelve BCE the Assyrians themselves were destroyed by a coalition of their enemies, as the Babylonians united with the Medians and the Scythians to conquer them. The Babylonians then inherited the Kingdom of Judah in Jerusalem. When the Israelites rebelled against their new King, he crushed them, destroying Solomon's Temple around five hundred and eighty-seven BCE. All of this had been foretold. After the Assyrian enslavement of the Northern Kingdom of Israel, the Prophet Nahum foretold Assyria's downfall, saying, in the Book of Nahum, chapter one, verse fourteen: "You will have no descendants to bear your name. I will destroy the images and idols that are in the temple of your gods. I will prepare your grave, for you are vile." His prophecy was fulfilled within decades. But the Babylonians then captured the Southern Kingdom of Judah, and exiled its people, who wept in captivity. As the Psalm records, in Psalm one hundred and thirty-seven, verse one: "By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat down, yea, we wept, when we remembered Zion." Their saviour would not be an Israelite, but a Zoroastrian; and the Bible would call him 'Messiah'. Into this turmoil came Cyrus, born around six hundred to five hundred and ninety BCE. In five hundred and fifty-nine BCE he inherited the kingdom of the small Persian state of Anshan, a vassal of the Medians led by a man who was potentially Cyrus's own grandfather, Astyages. Astyages was said to be a cruel ruler, whose policies bred great disaffection among the Median nobility. Cyrus overthrew him and united the kingdoms of Media and Persia under himself, and in doing so laid the foundation of the great Achaemenid Empire. Yet it would be the Holy Quran that would forever establish his divine support, through the enigmatic story of Dhul-Qarnain: the Two-Horned King.

verses.text: You will have no descendants to bear your name. I will destroy the images and idols that are in the temple of your gods. I will prepare your grave, for you are vile.
By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat down, yea, we wept, when we remembered Zion

the-cyrus-cylinder.script: The Cyrus Cylinder. Behind you stands a replica of the Cyrus Cylinder, inscribed around five hundred and thirty-nine BCE. In Cyrus's own voice it declares: "I am Cyrus, King of the world... When I entered Babylon, I did not allow anyone to terrorise the land. I strove for peace in Babylon and in all her cities... Marduk, the great lord, rejoiced at my good deeds, and he pronounced a sweet blessing over me, Cyrus, the king who fears him, and over Cambyses, the son my issue, and over all my troops, that we might live happily in his presence, in well-being." The cylinder records the conquest of Babylon in the native Akkadian tongue. Its first part is written in the third person, and presents Cyrus as the chosen servant of Marduk, Babylon's 'lord of all gods', sent to punish their previous leader, Nabonidus. The text then switches to a first-person account in the voice of Cyrus, which describes his being chosen by Marduk to enter Babylon in peace. It explains how he treated the people of Babylon with kindness, and how he restored the gods to their temples, so that they could be worshipped by their respective devotees. The text is significant, for it shows how Cyrus's conquest was received and understood by the Babylonian elite themselves, and it stands as one of the earliest surviving records of Cyrus's pioneering religious tolerance.

verses.text: I am Cyrus, King of the world... When I entered Babylon, I did not allow anyone to terrorise the land. I strove for peace in Babylon and in all her cities... Marduk, the great lord, rejoiced at my good deeds, and he pronounced a sweet blessing over me, Cyrus, the king who fears him, and over Cambyses, the son my issue, and over all my troops, that we might live happily in his presence, in well-being.

who-was-dhul-qarnain.script: Who Was Dhul-Qarnain? Here two ancient maps sit side by side: the empire of Alexander the Great, and the Achaemenid Persian Empire around five hundred BCE. Let us set the story in context. The eighteenth chapter of the Holy Quran outlines the evolution of the Christian world; from the righteous and persecuted followers of the Messiah, to great Christian powers falling prey to materialism. This story comes towards its end when it explains how Dhul-Qarnain stopped the entry of the marauding peoples of Gog and Magog from entering his lands. It would be the descendants of Gog and Magog who would become the great European powers of the Latter Days, colonising the world materially and spiritually. But who, then, was Dhul-Qarnain? Most Muslims believe he was Alexander the Great; the Caliphs of the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community, however, have proved that he was Cyrus the Great. Consider three clues. First, the title 'Dhul-Qarnain' means one who possesses two horns, or who spans two generations, two centuries. Cyrus unified two great kingdoms, the Median and the Persian, and his life spanned two generations, while Alexander died at the young age of just thirty-two. Second, the three journeys of Dhul-Qarnain line up with Cyrus's known conquests, which went west, then east; Alexander's conquests spread only east. As the map of Alexander's empire shows, his conquests began in Macedonia, near Greece, and expanded eastwards only, across Asia Minor, the Near East, and through into India and Afghanistan; he never had any westward or northern expansion to his empire. Cyrus, by contrast, rose to kingship in the fortieth year of his life. His first conquest was in five hundred and fifty BCE, of the Medes. He then proceeded westward, conquering Turkey in five hundred and forty-six BCE, before heading east to conquer through to Uzbekistan; and finally, the Quran indicates, he secured his northern border at the Caucasus mountains. And third, Cyrus the Great was a magnificent moral and religious figure, ushering in an unprecedented era of religious freedom. Alexander was a brilliant but profligate pagan conqueror; he cannot be described as the righteous figure of Dhul-Qarnain. The evidence, then, points firmly to Cyrus.
